title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Peru — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Café con leche is the morning ritual
items.text: Peruvians start their day with a large cup of weak espresso mixed with hot milk, often with a bread roll (pan). Ask for 'café con leche' at any café or even your hotel—it's cheap, ubiquitous, and fundamentally Peruvian.

items.label: Specialty coffee is a Lima thing
items.text: High-quality third-wave coffee shops are concentrated in Lima's Miraflores, Barranco, and San Isidro districts. Outside the capital, expect standard café or instant Nescafé; don't expect flat whites in Cusco or the Sacred Valley.

items.label: Café Café (Lima)
items.text: A reliable chain with good espresso drinks and pastries across Lima. It's tourist-friendly but actually serves competent coffee, unlike many tourist-targeted spots.

items.label: Café is served after meals, not with breakfast
items.text: Peruvians have coffee in the morning with bread, but 'café' (espresso) arrives after dinner. Don't expect automatic coffee service with your meal—order it specifically.

items.label: Ceviche
items.text: Raw fish cured in lime juice with red onions, cilantro, and chili. It's Peru's national dish—order it at lunch (never dinner) from a reputable restaurant near the coast, as freshness is critical.

items.label: Lomo saltado
items.text: Stir-fried beef with tomatoes, onions, and peppers, served with rice and fries. It's comfort food found everywhere and a reliable choice when uncertain.

items.label: Causa limeña
items.text: A cold terrine of mashed yellow potatoes layered with avocado, chicken, or seafood. It's a Lima specialty and perfect for warm days—look for it in coastal restaurants.

items.label: Ají de gallina
items.text: Shredded chicken in a creamy yellow chili sauce served over rice. It's homey and warming, especially good in the highlands, and typically inexpensive.

items.label: Anticuchos
items.text: Marinated and grilled beef heart skewers with potatoes and corn. Street vendors sell them late afternoon and evening—they're an authentic, affordable snack.

items.label: Papa a la huancaína
items.text: Boiled potatoes topped with a creamy yellow chili cheese sauce and olives. It's a classic starter and completely vegetarian, though some versions include chicken.

items.label: Chupe de camarones
items.text: A rich shrimp soup with potatoes, corn, and cream from Peru's coastal regions. It's hearty and often served with a raw egg yolk to stir in.

items.label: Menú del día is your budget friend
items.text: Most restaurants offer a fixed lunch menu (menú del día) with soup, main, dessert, and drink for 10–20 soles. It's cheaper than ordering à la carte and often better quality.

items.label: Ask what's fresh, not what's on the printed menu
items.text: In casual restaurants and at markets, ask 'Qué hay hoy?' (What do you have today?) rather than relying on the menu board. Fresh fish and seasonal items vary daily.

items.label: Portion sizes are large
items.text: A single main course often serves two people. Share dishes or ask for a media porción (half portion) if available, or expect significant leftovers.

items.label: Verify prices upfront at informal spots
items.text: At markets, street stalls, and small comedores (local eateries), no menu may be posted. Always ask the price before ordering to avoid surprises.

items.label: Agua sin gas is tap water (usually safe in cities)
items.text: In Lima and major cities, tap water is potable. Restaurants bring water automatically—specify 'sin gas' (still) or 'con gas' (sparkling) if you want bottled.

items.label: Mercados (markets) for authentic cheap eats
items.text: Seek out San Isidro or Santa Cruz markets for fresh ceviche, anticuchos, and regional dishes at 8–15 soles. Go at lunch when locals eat and food turns over quickly.

items.label: Comedores—tiny local eateries
items.text: Family-run hole-in-the-wall restaurants, often without signs, serve the menú del día to workers. They're cheap, genuine, and found in every neighborhood—ask locals to point you in.

items.label: Tourist restaurants in Sacred Valley charge premium prices
items.text: Restaurants with views of ruins or Ollantaytambo's fortress add 50–100% to prices. Head downhill into town or ask your hotel for local spots away from the tourist trail.

items.label: Lima's fine dining scene is world-class
items.text: Restaurants like Central, Maido, and Astrid y Gastón rank globally. Book weeks ahead; they're expensive but offer genuine haute Peruvian cuisine.

items.label: Avoid 'tourist menus' with photos and inflated prices
items.text: Laminated menus with pictures of food aimed at foreigners near major attractions signal tourist traps. Peruvians eat where there are no pictures and no English signs.

items.label: Barranco (Lima) for bohemian dining
items.text: This artsy neighborhood has honest mid-range restaurants, cevicherías, and cafés mixed with galleries and bookshops. It's touristy but genuinely vibrant, unlike the tourist monoculture of Plaza Mayor.

items.label: Pisco sour
items.text: Peru's national cocktail: grape brandy (pisco), lime juice, egg white, bitters, and sugar shaken into a frothy drink. Order it with ice; it's smooth but deceptively strong.

items.label: Chicha morada
items.text: A sweet, non-alcoholic drink made from purple corn, spices, and fruit. It's refreshing, unique to Peru, and served cold in markets and comedores.

items.label: Coca tea [té de coca]
items.text: Hot water steeped with coca leaves, served to ease altitude sickness in the highlands. It's mild, legal, and genuinely helps—order it at your Cusco hotel.

items.label: Peruvian beer is light and widely available
items.text: Cristal, Pilsen, and Cusqueña are the main brands—all light lagers. Local craft breweries exist in Lima, but industrial beer dominates outside the capital.

items.label: Inca Kola
items.text: A bright yellow, vanilla-flavored soda that's ubiquitous and uniquely Peruvian. It's overly sweet, but trying it is part of the experience.

items.label: Wine culture is growing but not traditional
items.text: Peru produces wine in southern valleys, but wine isn't as embedded in dining as in Chile or Argentina. Most restaurants offer basic wine lists; beer and pisco are more natural choices.

items.label: Street juice stands are fresh but potentially risky
items.text: Jugo stands blend tropical fruits daily—papaya, mango, passion fruit. The fruit is excellent, but water quality is uncertain; buy where there's high volume and locals queue.

items.label: Lunch (1–3 PM) is the main meal; dinner is light
items.text: Peruvians eat their largest meal at midday, then have a light supper (cena) around 8 PM. Restaurants fill at lunch; dinner service is often slow because fewer people eat heartily at night.

items.label: Tipping is expected but not automatic
items.text: In formal restaurants, leave 10% if service was good; in casual spots, 5% or loose change is fine. Credit card machines often ask for tips (propina)—decline if service was poor.

items.label: Peruvians eat slowly and socially
items.text: Meals are leisurely events, not rushed refuelings. Lingering for an hour or more is normal; rushing through signals rudeness. Don't expect quick table turnover.

items.label: Bread and butter arrive automatically—you'll be charged
items.text: Pan y mantequilla appear unsolicited at the table. If you eat it, you'll pay (usually 1–3 soles). Push it aside if you don't want the charge.

items.label: Say 'buen provecho' when locals are eating
items.text: It's the standard greeting in Peru equivalent to 'bon appétit.' Use it when passing someone eating or when served, and locals will respond warmly.

items.label: Ask for la cuenta (the bill) explicitly—it won't come unprompted
items.text: Servers leave you alone after eating unless you signal. Say 'la cuenta, por favor' or raise your hand—they won't rush you off.

sections.title: Markets & Street Food [Mercados y Comida Callejera]

items.label: Mercado Central (Lima)
items.text: Lima's largest food market is organized chaos with fresh produce, seafood, and cooked food stalls. Arrive before 1 PM for lunch; the atmosphere is intense but authentic.

items.label: Eat where locals queue, not where vendors call out
items.text: At markets, the busiest stalls have high turnover and lower food-safety risk. Avoid empty stalls and always watch your food being prepared.

items.label: Empanadas and pasteles
items.text: Hand-held fried pockets filled with meat, cheese, or vegetables sold by street vendors. They're cheap (2–4 soles), filling, and widely safe if eaten fresh from the fryer.

items.label: Choclo con queso
items.text: Roasted large-kernel corn (choclo) served with fresh cheese, sold at street stalls throughout the country. It's simple, nutritious, and a classic snack.

items.label: Ceviche from street vendors is riskier than restaurants
items.text: While market ceviche can be excellent, fish freshness and ice quality vary wildly. Save ceviche for established restaurants unless you're confident in the vendor's reputation.
